content.text: What it is

Standard dashboard templates are pre-approved, parameterized dashboard definitions — typically Grafana JSON or Grafonnet (Jsonnet) files — that every agent team instantiates rather than builds from scratch. Instead of each team authoring panels from a blank canvas, the observability platform team maintains a curated library of template files that encode the correct datasource connections, panel layout, query structure, and alert thresholds for a given service archetype.
A template is not a static file duplicated per team. It is a
parameterized definition

that accepts variables — service name, environment, model provider, time range — so that a single template file, rendered at deploy time, produces a correctly-scoped dashboard for any team that instantiates it. The rendering step is typically part of the dashboard-as-code pipeline: Terraform, a Grafana provisioning YAML, or a CI script calls a Grafonnet or Jsonnet build step to stamp out the final dashboard JSON per tenant.
Why it matters

Agent teams share a common observability vocabulary — request rate, error rate, latency percentiles, token usage, tool call success, eval quality, guardrail activation — but they express it inconsistently when left to author dashboards ad hoc. The result is a fleet of dashboards that look different, query different metric names, use different axis units, and hide the same golden signals behind team-specific naming conventions.

When every team instantiates the same template, an incident responder who has never seen a particular agent's service before can navigate its dashboard with zero ramp-up time. The panels are where they are expected to be, the thresholds are consistently defined, and the labels use the organisation-wide naming convention. This matters most at 2 AM during a cross-service incident when an on-call engineer is jumping between five dashboards at once.
Benefits
Consistency: the same panel names, units, and layout across every agent service — engineers spend zero time orienting to an unfamiliar dashboard during incidents.
Onboarding speed: a new team adds a single Terraform variable block or provisioning YAML stanza and gets a complete, correct dashboard on day one.

Governance: golden signals (request rate, error rate, latency, saturation) plus agent-specific signals (tool call success rate, eval score, guardrail activation) are guaranteed to be visible without any team configuration.
Maintainability: when a metric is renamed or a panel needs a threshold change, the platform team updates one template file and the change propagates to all instantiated dashboards on the next deploy.
Audit compliance: every dashboard is produced from a version-controlled source, so you can prove at any point what a dashboard showed and when it changed.
Trade-offs

Flexibility cost: teams with genuinely unusual panels — specialised retrieval visualisations, custom eval histograms — must either extend the template system or maintain divergent panels outside the template. Divergence should be tracked and reviewed.

Template sprawl: without curation, template libraries grow unbounded. Each new template category is a maintenance commitment. Start with the fewest templates that cover the common cases and add only on demonstrated need.

Abstraction leakage: Jsonnet and Grafonnet templating add a build step and a new language to learn. Teams not familiar with functional Jsonnet may find the template system harder to debug than raw JSON.
Template versioning: when the template changes, teams that pinned to an old version may not automatically receive the update. Pin management and migration planning are required.
How it works
A Grafana template variable is a top-level field in the dashboard JSON that instructs Grafana to render a dropdown at the top of the dashboard. Query panels reference the variable using the
${variable_name}

syntax. Every PromQL, ClickHouse, or Datadog query in every panel is written in terms of these variables so that selecting a different service name from the dropdown instantly re-scopes the entire dashboard without touching any panel configuration.

At provision time, the template build step substitutes default values for the variables and writes the resulting JSON to a provisioning folder that Grafana watches. Grafana's built-in file provisioner reloads dashboards from that folder on startup and can be configured to reload on file change, enabling GitOps-style dashboard deploys: a CI merge to main triggers a build of the dashboard JSON, commits it to the provisioning folder, and Grafana picks up the change without a restart.
Architecture
The template architecture has three layers:
Template source (Grafonnet/Jsonnet): parameterised functions that return Grafana panel or row objects. Stored in a shared library repo, versioned with semver tags.
Instance definition (YAML or Terraform): each team's stanza that names which template to use and provides values for the required variables (service_name, environment, datasource, model_provider).
Rendered output (dashboard JSON): the build step produces one JSON file per dashboard instance, committed to the provisioning directory and synced to Grafana.

attrs.alt: Agent dashboard template anatomy: variable bar with $service_name, $environment, $time_range, $model_provider, followed by three rows of panels covering golden signals, agent-specific signals, and cost/provider metrics

content.text: When to use it
When more than two agent teams exist in the organisation — at that point, dashboard divergence becomes a real coordination cost during incidents.
When your on-call rotation spans multiple teams — engineers need consistent navigation across dashboards they did not author.
When your dashboards are already version-controlled (dashboards-as-code) — templates are the natural next step to share structure across repositories.
When new agent services are being launched frequently — templates compress onboarding time for observability from days to under an hour.
How to implement
Audit existing dashboards across teams. Identify the panels that appear in every agent dashboard — these become the mandatory rows in the template.
Define the variable contract. Settle on four to six variables: service_name, environment, datasource, model_provider, time_range, and optionally cluster.

Write the template in Grafonnet or raw Jsonnet. Each row is a function returning a list of panel objects. Export a top-level function that accepts the variable contract and returns a complete dashboard object.
Add a CI step that renders the template for each registered service and validates the output with grafana-dashboard-linter. Fail the PR if required panels are missing.
Provision rendered JSON to Grafana via the file provisioner or Terraform grafana_dashboard resources. Organise dashboards under a folder per template category.
Examples

Grafonnet row definition for the golden-signals row:
Real-world example

A fintech company operating eight agent services — loan underwriting assistant, document extraction pipeline, fraud-signal aggregator, customer FAQ bot, trade-confirmation drafter, KYC verifier, portfolio summariser, and compliance checker — authored eight independent Grafana dashboards over six months. Each team used different metric names for the same concept (e.g.,
requests_total
vs
http_requests
vs
agent_calls
). During a cross-service incident, the on-call engineer spent 40 minutes finding the equivalent panels across dashboards before diagnosing the root cause.

The observability platform team standardised on OpenTelemetry semantic conventions for metric names, authored a Grafonnet agent template with four mandatory rows (golden signals, agent-specific, cost, and provider), and migrated all eight dashboards to be instantiations of the template. The next cross-service incident was diagnosed in under eight minutes because every dashboard had the same panel layout and every metric name was identical.
Common mistakes
Copying dashboard JSON instead of instantiating the template. Copies diverge immediately. Treat any duplicate dashboard JSON as a code smell requiring template extraction.
Hard-coding service names in panel queries instead of using template variables. This breaks the parameterisation and makes the dashboard single-tenant.
Using Grafana's UI to edit provisioned dashboards. Changes made in the UI are ephemeral — the next provision cycle overwrites them. All edits must go through the source template.

Omitting agent-specific panels from the template and leaving them as team responsibility. This recreates the inconsistency problem. The eval quality score, tool call success rate, guardrail activation rate, and token usage panels must be in the template, not optional.
Not versioning the template library. Without semver tags on the library, a breaking change to a panel query silently breaks all dashboards on the next build.
Best practices

Organise templates into exactly four categories to cover the common cases without proliferation: (1) Agent Overview, (2) Tool Performance, (3) Quality/Eval, (4) Cost/Token. Most agent teams need all four; some need only the first one.

Keep the mandatory panel set small. A template with 30 required panels is harder to instantiate correctly than one with 8. Optional rows for specialised views are fine — use a collapse-by-default row.
Test the template rendering in CI against a synthetic Prometheus scrape target. Confirm that each panel returns non-empty data for a known test service before merging template changes.

Document the variable contract in a README adjacent to the template source. Every engineer instantiating the template should know exactly which variables are required, which are optional, and what the expected value format is.
Maintain a changelog for the template library. Teams need to know what changed between template versions to plan migrations without surprises.
Performance and scalability

Template-based dashboards scale better than ad hoc ones in two dimensions. First, query performance: because every team uses the same PromQL structure, the observability platform team can apply a single recording rule (e.g.,
job:agent_request_total:rate5m
) that pre-computes the time series for all services. Ad hoc dashboards with varied query structures cannot benefit from a shared recording rule.

Second, render performance: Grafana loads a template-derived dashboard in a predictable number of queries because the panel count is fixed by the template. An ad hoc dashboard that grows organically often accumulates redundant panels that fire duplicate queries. With template governance, the panel count stays bounded and each panel does exactly one job.
At very large scale (50+ services), consider
meta-dashboards

— fleet-level dashboards that aggregate the key metrics across all agent services using the same metric names standardised by the template. Meta-dashboards are trivial to build when all services share a naming convention and impossible when they do not.
Tooling and ecosystem
Grafonnet
: the official Grafana Jsonnet library. Provides typed builder functions for every Grafana panel type, reducing the chance of malformed JSON.
grafana-dashboard-linter
: a CLI tool that validates dashboard JSON against Grafana's schema and checks for common panel mistakes. Integrate into CI as a PR gate.
Grafana Terraform provider
: the
grafana_dashboard
resource provisions a dashboard from a JSON string or file, fitting naturally into infrastructure-as-code pipelines.
Grafana file provisioner
: a native Grafana feature that watches a directory for JSON files and loads/reloads them automatically, enabling GitOps-style deploys without the Terraform dependency.
mixtool / jsonnet-bundler
: package management for Jsonnet libraries. Use
jb install
to pin Grafonnet to a specific version and vendor it into your template repository.
Comparison with alternatives
Three organisational strategies exist for dashboard creation in agent teams, with distinct trade-offs:
No standard (ad hoc per team)

: fastest to start, but divergence compounds quickly. Incident response time increases with the number of services because every dashboard is a foreign environment. Not viable beyond two or three teams.
Shared static dashboard (all services on one dashboard)
: consistent but does not scale. A single dashboard with 20 services and 200 panels is slow to load and hard to filter. Template-per-team is preferable because each dashboard is service-scoped.
Shared template library (recommended)

: each team gets a scoped, service-specific dashboard that is structurally identical to every other team's dashboard. The template library is the single source of truth for panel definitions and metric queries.
Security and reliability
Dashboard templates have two security-relevant properties. First,
access control by folder

: Grafana folder permissions govern who can view and edit dashboards. Template-provisioned dashboards are assigned to named folders by the provisioner, which means access control is enforced at provisioning time, not left to manual Grafana UI configuration that can be misconfigured.
Second,
datasource access

: templates reference datasources by UID or variable. Provisioned datasources should use service-account credentials with read-only access to the metrics backend. This prevents a dashboard-level SQL injection — where a user manipulates a template variable to run an arbitrary query — from having write access to the backing store.

Reliability: because templates are rendered and validated in CI before deploy, a malformed query is caught before it reaches production Grafana. This prevents the dashboard-down scenario where a UI-edited panel with a typo breaks the dashboard for every on-call engineer who opens it during an incident.
Mental model
Think of a standard dashboard template as a
class
in object-oriented terms. The template defines structure and behaviour (which panels to show, what queries to run, what thresholds to apply). A team's instantiation is an
instance

of that class, parameterised with the team's specific service name and environment. Just as you do not copy a class definition for each instantiation, you do not copy a dashboard JSON file for each service.

The template library is the observability equivalent of a component library in a frontend codebase — shared, versioned, peer-reviewed, and consumed via dependency management rather than copy-paste. When the design system updates a button, every app gets the update. When the template library updates a query, every dashboard gets the fix.
Key takeaways
Standard dashboard templates are parameterised dashboard definitions that every agent team instantiates, not copies. One source of truth drives all dashboards.

Every agent dashboard must include at minimum: request rate, error rate, latency p50/p95/p99, token saturation, tool call success rate, eval quality score, guardrail activation rate, and model provider latency.
Four template variables handle most parameterisation needs: $service_name, $environment, $time_range, $model_provider.
Use Grafonnet (Jsonnet) for authoring templates, grafana-dashboard-linter for CI validation, and Grafana's file provisioner or Terraform for deployment.
Four template categories cover the common cases: Agent Overview, Tool Performance, Quality/Eval, Cost/Token.
Template discipline pays its largest dividend during cross-service incidents, where consistent dashboard layout collapses the time needed to navigate an unfamiliar service's observability surface.
